ancestors.notes: Descent from Old Indo-Aryan (Sanskrit) via Sauraseni Prakrit and Apabhramsa to the Delhi/Khari Boli of the Western Hindi area.
Dravidian (and Munda) substrate widely credited with Indo-Aryan retroflexion (the ʈ ɖ ɳ series) and convergence features.
Persian adstrate: dominant administrative/prestige language of the Delhi Sultanate and Mughal empire; the Perso-Arabic layer that defines the Hindustani register continuum.
Arabic adstrate: religious/legal vocabulary borrowed largely through Persian.

name: Hindi
notes: Standard Hindi (Khaṛī Bolī). 4-way laryngeal contrast: voiceless, aspirated, voiced, breathy-voiced. Nukta letters for Perso-Arabic loans may not be distinguished by all speakers. SCHWA DELETION: inherent schwa deleted word-finally and in medial CVC patterns (Pandey 2014). ड/ढ FLAPPING: retroflex stops [ɖ/ɖʱ] become flaps [ɽ/ɽʱ] intervocalically. 4-WAY LARYNGEAL: voiceless unaspirated, voiceless aspirated, voiced, breathy-voiced stops. DENTALS vs RETROFLEXES: dental series (त/थ/द/ध) vs. retroflex (ट/ठ/ड/ढ) fully contrastive. Sources: Ohala (1983), Pandey (2014), Masica (1991), Shapiro (2003).

sources.author: Shapiro, M.

sources.author: Ohala, M.

sources.notes: Chapter 5 states the schwa-deletion rule and its VC_CV environment; the source for HI_SCHWA_*.

sources.author: Ohala, M.
sources.title: Hindi
sources.publisher: in Handbook of the International Phonetic Association, Cambridge University Press
sources.notes: IPA illustration of Hindi; the consonant and vowel inventory and the note that the inherent vowel is not pronounced word-finally.

sources.author: Narasimhan, B., Sproat, R. & Kiraz, G.
sources.title: Schwa-Deletion in Hindi Text-to-Speech Synthesis
sources.notes: Computational statement of the rule used here: delete schwa in VC_CV, applied right-to-left; the source for HI_SCHWA_MEDIAL_* and its HI_SCHWA_KEEP_* guard.

allophone_rules.notes: Word-final schwa deletion. Hindi does not pronounce the inherent vowel of a word-final consonant letter: राम is [ɾaːm], not *[ɾaːmə] (Ohala 1983, ch. 5; Ohala 1999: 101; Wikipedia, 'Schwa deletion in Indo-Aryan languages'). Applies after a cluster too (शब्द [ʃəbd̪]), so it is ordered BEFORE the HI_SCHWA_KEEP_* guard. word_initial=false protects a monosyllabic one-letter word (न [nə]), whose schwa is the only vowel and is never deleted. Sanskrit (sa, sa-x-vedic) preserves the inherent vowel and declares no such rule — which is why this lives in the Hindi DATA, not in the engine.
Word-final schwa deletion; see HI_SCHWA_FINAL_b (Ohala 1983, ch. 5; Ohala 1999: 100-103).

Guard for the VC_CV medial rule (Ohala 1983; Narasimhan, Sproat & Kiraz 2004): the schwa is deleted only when a VOWEL precedes the consonant that carries it. This identity rewrite fires first when the previous lattice slot is a vowel-less consonant (a cluster member, i.e. a consonant carrying a virama), and — because the first matching rule wins — blocks HI_SCHWA_MEDIAL_*: प्रकार is [pɾəkaːɾ], never *[pɾkaːɾ].
Guard for the VC_CV medial rule; see HI_SCHWA_KEEP_b (Ohala 1983, ch. 5).

Medial schwa deletion in the VC_CV environment, the classic Hindi generalisation (Ohala 1983, ch. 5; Narasimhan, Sproat & Kiraz 2004: 319-333): a schwa is deleted when it is preceded by a vowel-final segment and the FOLLOWING consonant itself carries a vowel — नमकीन [nəmkiːn], सरकार [səɾkaːɾ], एतवार [eːt̪ʋaːɾ]. The condition 'the following consonant carries a vowel of its own' is read straight off the lattice: a consonant whose inherent vowel was cancelled by a following matra surfaces as a BARE consonant phoneme, so followed_by_phoneme = the vowel-less consonant class is exactly that test. A following consonant that still carries its own inherent schwa (कमल: ल = [lə]) does NOT license deletion, which reproduces the right-to-left application of the rule without iterating: कमल [kəməl], not *[kəml] — the neighbour view is the lattice as it stood BEFORE this pass, so the word-final deletion on ल is invisible here.
Medial VC_CV schwa deletion; see HI_SCHWA_MEDIAL_b (Narasimhan, Sproat & Kiraz 2004: 319-333).
